case_name: Hexavalent chromium removal from small well-water system for drinking water
task_description: Recommend a ranked list of feasible treatment process chains for chromium-contaminated well water intended for drinking water supply under low-energy small-system constraints.

input_data.scenario_background: A small rural drinking water system uses a deep well as its only source and has detected elevated chromium in the raw water. The utility needs a proven treatment approach that can reliably meet drinking water requirements without high energy demand or highly specialized operations. The operator capacity is limited to routine chemical dosing, cartridge or pressure filter maintenance, and periodic media replacement or regeneration oversight.

treatment_goal.target_requirement: Finished water should comply with drinking water chromium requirements with stable routine performance for a small community supply.

engineering_constraints.residuals_management: limited residuals handling capacity; periodic spent media replacement or manageable regeneration waste is acceptable, but large sludge volumes or membrane concentrate are undesirable
engineering_constraints.robustness_requirement: consistent compliance with simple and reliable operation is required

core_unit_functions.anion_exchange: selectively remove dissolved hexavalent chromium oxyanions from low-turbidity groundwater
core_unit_functions.post_filtration: capture resin fines and provide final particulate polishing
core_unit_functions.disinfection: maintain microbiological safety in the distribution system

applicability_rationale: hexavalent chromium is the dominant chromium species and is well suited to anion exchange treatment
the raw well water has very low turbidity and low iron and manganese, which supports direct fixed-bed treatment
anion exchange is a widely recognized small-system approach for Cr(VI) control in groundwater
the process has very low energy demand and fits the utility preference for conventional packaged treatment
potential_risks: sulfate and other competing anions can shorten resin run length
breakthrough monitoring is essential to prevent chromium exceedance
if on-site regeneration is used, brine waste handling must be controlled

core_unit_functions.reduction: convert Cr(VI) to Cr(III), which is less mobile and easier to remove
core_unit_functions.iron_based_adsorptive_media_or_coprecipi: adsorb or coprecipitate reduced chromium with ferric hydroxide solids or iron media surfaces
core_unit_functions.filtration: remove precipitated solids or media fines

applicability_rationale: reduction followed by iron-based removal is a well-established chromium treatment concept
the water has low natural solids, so a compact filtration-based system is feasible
this option avoids high-pressure membranes and remains relatively low energy
it can be attractive when a non-regenerable media or simple chemical reduction approach is preferred
potential_risks: incomplete reduction of Cr(VI) will reduce overall treatment reliability
chemical dosing control and oxidation-reduction monitoring are required
solid residuals or spent media need compliant disposal

core_unit_functions.reduction: chemically reduce dissolved Cr(VI) to Cr(III)
core_unit_functions.coagulation: form metal hydroxide flocs that capture and precipitate chromium
core_unit_functions.pressure_filtration: separate formed solids in a compact small-system format

applicability_rationale: this is a conventional and technically proven route for chromium removal after speciation control
pressure filtration can be more compact than full sedimentation systems for a small utility
the approach is robust when chemical feed and filtration are properly operated
potential_risks: produces more sludge than ion exchange or simple adsorptive systems
performance depends on pH and coagulant dose optimization
small systems may find solids handling and backwash management burdensome

core_unit_functions.adsorptive_media: remove chromium through adsorption onto specialized media under favorable water chemistry
core_unit_functions.post_filtration: retain particulate carryover and polish effluent

applicability_rationale: packaged adsorptive systems can be simple and low energy for small well-water applications
this route may be practical where validated media are available for the site-specific chromium species and competing ions
it can reduce operator burden compared with coagulation systems
potential_risks: performance is highly media-specific and may be sensitive to sulfate and pH
not all adsorptive media provide reliable long-run Cr(VI) capacity
pilot testing or vendor validation is advisable before full-scale selection

core_unit_functions.reverse_osmosis: reject dissolved chromium and other ions through a high-pressure membrane barrier
core_unit_functions.stabilization: adjust corrosivity and mineral balance of permeate
core_unit_functions.disinfection: ensure safe finished water for distribution

applicability_rationale: reverse osmosis can achieve strong chromium removal
it may be considered if broader dissolved contaminant reduction is also needed beyond chromium alone
potential_risks: high energy demand conflicts with the stated low-energy requirement
membrane concentrate disposal is difficult for a small inland well system
greater operator skill, pretreatment needs, and maintenance burden make it less suitable for this straightforward case

preferred_rank_pattern: anion_exchange for hexavalent chromium in low-turbidity well water should rank first
reduction plus iron-based removal or coagulation-filtration should rank above high-pressure membranes
reverse_osmosis should not rank first under low-energy small-system constraints

must_reflect: low-energy preference should favor ion exchange or other compact adsorption-based systems
small-system operator capacity should favor proven and manageable treatment trains
residuals and concentrate burdens should be considered when ranking alternatives

must_contain: the importance of chromium speciation, especially Cr(VI) versus Cr(III)
why anion exchange is appropriate for dissolved hexavalent chromium in clean well water
why reduction is needed before precipitation-based removal routes
why reverse osmosis ranks lower despite technical effectiveness

should_mention: competing sulfate effects on anion exchange capacity
breakthrough monitoring needs for resin or media systems
waste brine, sludge, or spent media disposal considerations
the need for complete reduction when using chemical reduction pathways
